counterexample_tests: Many structured families are classically resolved by support splitting or stronger invariants.
Hardness of measuring a coset state is not evidence that the underlying code family is classically hard.
A solver for a restricted code family does not establish a route from general code equivalence.
certificate.decoder_description: A permutation P, and then S by polynomial-time linear algebra.
certificate.hardness_assumption: The stated search code-equivalence problem, not only a selected algebraic code family.
certificate.mapping_description: Uniformly construct: A coherently evaluable group-action hiding function derived from both generator matrices.
certificate.oracle_translation: The reduction supplies these target capabilities: coherent-hiding-function-evaluation, coset-state-preparation.
certificate.parameter_map: Group representation and oracle evaluation are polynomial in n, k, and log q.
certificate.preprocessing_and_advice: Uniform construction from M and M'; no private code-family advice.
certificate.promise_mapping: Source scope: The stated search code-equivalence problem, not only a selected algebraic code family. Target contract: Recover the hidden subgroup/action element sufficiently to output the coordinate permutation P.
certificate.source_input_model: Two full-rank generator matrices M and M' for equivalent q-ary linear codes, with M'=SMP.
certificate.success_statement: Bounded-error recovery on every promised equivalent code pair in the claimed scope.
certificate.target_input_model: Recover the hidden subgroup/action element sufficiently to output the coordinate permutation P.

counterexample_tests: This is not a reduction from exact SVP or arbitrary approximation factors.
It supplies coset-state samples, not automatically a public reversible evaluator for a phase family.
The f=1 theorem permits arbitrary bad basis-state registers at probability up to 1/log N; a perfect-state sieve does not cover the theorem input.
Hardness does not transfer to a restricted easy dihedral or hidden-shift subfamily without another reduction.
certificate.decoder_description: A vector satisfying the paper's unique-SVP approximation promise.
certificate.hardness_assumption: Worst-case promised lattice instances in the stated approximation regime.
certificate.mapping_description: Uniformly construct: poly(log N) tensor-product registers with a common hidden slope d. Each register is a valid dihedral coset state with probability at least 1-1/(log N)^f and otherwise may be an arbitrary basis state |b,x|.
certificate.oracle_translation: The reduction supplies these target capabilities: independent-coset-state-samples, quantum-state-input, adversarial-bad-registers-at-rate-1-over-logN-to-f.
certificate.parameter_map: In the improved proof choose prime p>n^(2+2f), M=2^(4n), and map the n-dimensional two-point instance to DCP modulus N=(2M)^n, hence log N=Theta(n^2). The f=1 approximation factor is Theta(n^2.5); the DCP input uses poly(log N) registers.
certificate.preprocessing_and_advice: Uniform construction from the lattice instance; no family-specific nonuniform advice.
certificate.promise_mapping: Source scope: Worst-case promised lattice instances in the stated approximation regime. Target contract: For f=1, output the common hidden slope d with probability poly(1/log N) in time poly(log N), despite per-register bad probability at most 1/log N. A perfect-state or restricted-family solver is insufficient.
certificate.source_input_model: The f=1 specialization of the paper's theorem: an n-dimensional lattice with a Theta(n^(1/2+2f))=Theta(n^2.5) unique-shortest-vector gap.
certificate.success_statement: Output d with probability poly(1/log N) and time poly(log N) under the DCP failure-parameter promise; the lattice routine then succeeds with inverse-polynomial probability and polynomial repetition.
certificate.target_input_model: For f=1, output the common hidden slope d with probability poly(1/log N) in time poly(log N), despite per-register bad probability at most 1/log N. A perfect-state or restricted-family solver is insufficient.

counterexample_tests: Construct an upstream hard instance outside the candidate family or show that the candidate promise makes it classically easy.
Attempt to preserve the source oracle and distribution through the proposed family specialization.
certificate.decoder_description: Candidate measurement/decoder is not proved to solve the full upstream target family.
certificate.hardness_assumption: Use the exact THM-REGEV-USVP-TO-DCP-2003 contract. A speedup claim requires full-family coverage, a uniform lattice-dimension/modulus/sample/precision map, and bounded-error composition with the lattice decoder.
certificate.mapping_description: No construction currently maps arbitrary upstream target instances into the candidate's restricted family.
certificate.oracle_translation: Unknown: coherent or coset-state access may not survive specialization to the candidate family.
certificate.parameter_map: Unknown: candidate-family parameters are not proved to cover the upstream target distribution.
certificate.preprocessing_and_advice: Unknown whether family generation, advice, or preprocessing preserves the upstream model.
certificate.promise_mapping: Unknown: the restricted algebraic/graph/code promise may discard hard upstream instances.
certificate.source_input_model: All standard dihedral-coset-problem instances covered by the upstream natural-problem reduction.
certificate.success_statement: Target theorem: on every DCP state instance in the Regev contract, recover the complete hidden reflection with bounded error while proving an asymptotic sample, time, or memory improvement over a named Kuperberg/Regev baseline and preserving the end-to-end lattice approximation guarantee.
certificate.target_input_model: Independent coset-state samples from D_N under the exact f=1 DCP promise: each register is good with probability at least 1-1/log N and otherwise may be an arbitrary basis-state register. Good registers transform into known uniformly random labels k and phase states. No evaluator or chosen labels are available.

certificate.decoder_description: A vertex permutation mapping one input graph to the other.
certificate.hardness_assumption: All explicitly represented graph pairs, not only CFI, regular, or cospectral subfamilies.
certificate.mapping_description: Uniformly construct: A coherently evaluable hiding function whose hidden subgroup encodes an isomorphism as an involution.
certificate.oracle_translation: The reduction supplies these target capabilities: coherent-hiding-function-evaluation, coset-state-preparation.
certificate.parameter_map: The target permutation degree and oracle evaluation cost are polynomial in graph size.
certificate.preprocessing_and_advice: Uniformly constructed from the two input graphs with no graph-family advice.
certificate.promise_mapping: Source scope: All explicitly represented graph pairs, not only CFI, regular, or cospectral subfamilies. Target contract: Recover enough of the hidden subgroup on every encoded graph pair to output and verify an isomorphism.
certificate.source_input_model: Two explicitly represented n-vertex graphs, with search output required on isomorphic inputs.
certificate.success_statement: Bounded-error recovery on the full encoded graph-isomorphism promise.
certificate.target_input_model: Recover enough of the hidden subgroup on every encoded graph pair to output and verify an isomorphism.

counterexample_tests: Many structured families are classically resolved by support splitting or stronger invariants.
Hardness of measuring a coset state is not evidence that the underlying code family is classically hard.
A solver for a restricted code family does not establish a route from general code equivalence.
certificate.decoder_description: A permutation P, and then S by polynomial-time linear algebra.
certificate.hardness_assumption: The stated search code-equivalence problem, not only a selected algebraic code family.
certificate.mapping_description: Uniformly construct: A coherently evaluable group-action hiding function derived from both generator matrices.
certificate.oracle_translation: The reduction supplies these target capabilities: coherent-hiding-function-evaluation, coset-state-preparation.
certificate.parameter_map: Group representation and oracle evaluation are polynomial in n, k, and log q.
certificate.preprocessing_and_advice: Uniform construction from M and M'; no private code-family advice.
certificate.promise_mapping: Source scope: The stated search code-equivalence problem, not only a selected algebraic code family. Target contract: Recover the hidden subgroup/action element sufficiently to output the coordinate permutation P.
certificate.source_input_model: Two full-rank generator matrices M and M' for equivalent q-ary linear codes, with M'=SMP.
certificate.success_statement: Bounded-error recovery on every promised equivalent code pair in the claimed scope.
certificate.target_input_model: Recover the hidden subgroup/action element sufficiently to output the coordinate permutation P.
